title: Survival Kit
blurb: The 20 phrases that get you through day one. Tap ▶ to hear it; tap ★ to save offline.

phrases.use: Get attention, apologize, or thank someone for a small favor — the single most useful word in Japan.
To shop staff, servers, anyone you don't know. Drop 'gozaimasu' only with close friends.
Yes/no are simple. Real 'no' often comes through as 'chigaimasu' (that's different/wrong) to save face.
The all-purpose request word. Point, say this, get what you want. Works for almost any situation.

phrases.en: I'm okay / No, thank you / It's fine
phrases.use: Doubles as a polite refusal — 'daijoubu desu' to pushy free samples, last-minute offers, extra side dishes.
phrases.note: DIE-joh-bu DES. Long-O shown in romaji = long sound (spell check helps).

phrases.use: When someone explains in Japanese and you don't get it. Add 'nihongo ga' to specify: 'I don't understand Japanese.'
First line at a hotel desk or tourist info when you don't see English signage.
phrases.note: 'o' between words is a particle (object marker) — see grammar.

phrases.use: When someone is speaking too fast to catch.
phrases.note: YOO-koo-ree. Doubled consonant = hold the sound.

phrases.use: Ask for a repeat. Pairs with 'mou ichido itte kudasai' if you want them to repeat a specific thing.
Stronger than sumimasen. Use when you actually did something — bumped someone, broke something.
Until ~10am. With friends: 'ohayou' without gozaimasu.
Daytime greeting. The 'wa' in 'konnichiwa' is the topic particle written 'ha' (see grammar).
After sunset. Same spelling/sound quirk as konnichiwa.
Only when meeting someone for the first time. Often followed by your name.
Real emergency. Loud voice attracts bystanders who will help.
Works everywhere — restaurants, train stations, parks. Bonus: pointed alone gets you shown.
Pointing at an unfamiliar menu item or dish and getting a real answer.
Point and ask. Pair with the Numbers section to understand the answer.
Instant context-setter. People will either switch to English or gesture more carefully.

phrases.romaji: shashin o totte mo ii desu ka?
phrases.use: Always ask at shrines, in private shops, when photographing staff, food, or interiors.
phrases.note: Shah-SHIN oh TOOT-teh moh EE des ka.
